morning.notes: Run to the park, 25 pull-ups, back by 7:40. No email until 8:30. This is the protocol and it works.

deep_work.focus: Exploratory reading — AI and knowledge work, looking for the next book question
deep_work.notes: Read three papers and two chapters. Taking notes in the research log. The AI-and-knowledge-worker territory is crowded and I'm skeptical of my own interest in it — am I genuinely curious or just following the obvious next topic? The question I keep returning to is about what happens to expertise when the tools change. That might be something.
deep_work.question_statement_progress: Draft of one paragraph. Not ready.

research_paper.notes: Two hours in the afternoon on the distributed systems draft. The proof is almost clean. One more pass on the boundary conditions section. Target: submitted to the conference by end of month.

new_yorker.notes: Pitch accepted on the slow productivity piece — a follow-up to the original. Deadline March 15. Manageable alongside the semester.

teaching.notes: Graduate seminar went well. One student asked whether slow productivity is compatible with a PhD timeline. Good question with a complicated answer. The system can be deliberately slow; the calendar cannot always accommodate it. I said so.

family.notes: Home by 5:15. Shutdown ritual at 5pm — 'schedule shutdown complete' — and I meant it today. Dinner, homework logistics, the usual middle-school chaos. Good evening.

time_block_review.adherence: high — green blocks held except one meeting that ran long
time_block_review.note: The meeting that ran long was mine. I need to enforce endings as well as beginnings.

at_risk: Next book question still unfocused — need to sharpen in the next two weeks
note: The AI territory pulls because it's the obvious follow-on to Slow Productivity. Obvious follow-ons produce obvious books. I'm not ready to commit until I have a question that surprises me.
